answers.value:
I feel that I have a very clear understanding of the purpose of my Plan of Care (PoC) meeting. From the beginning, staff explained that this meeting is specifically to review how I have been doing over the past 90 days, make sure my services match my current needs, and adjust anything that is not working well. They also made it clear that this is my opportunity to speak up about what is helping, what feels overwhelming, and what I would like to work on next.

For example, I understand that the PoC is not just a general check-in but a formal document that guides my home health visits, physical therapy schedule, medication management support, and transportation services to medical appointments. During my first meeting, my case manager, Ellen, went over the plan section by section—medical needs, mobility, mental health, daily living activities, and social support. At this 90-day follow-up, I knew we were revisiting those same areas to see if my goals (like increasing how far I can walk with my walker, and managing my anxiety when leaving the house) are still accurate and realistic.

I also understand that the PoC meeting is where we agree on who is responsible for what—for example, what the nurse will monitor, what the physical therapist will focus on, and what I am expected to do on my own at home (like my daily leg exercises and tracking my blood pressure readings). Knowing this helps me feel more organized and less confused about why certain people come to my home and what they are supposed to be helping me with.

In short, I see the PoC meeting as the main place where my care is coordinated and updated, and where my voice is supposed to guide those decisions. That has been explained more than once, in plain language, which I appreciate.

answers.value:
The PoC and the PoC meetings have been very beneficial to my care because they help connect all the different services I receive into something that feels like one plan instead of a bunch of separate appointments. Before I started this program, I felt like I was juggling too many things on my own—primary care doctor, cardiologist, mental health counselor, and physical therapy at different places, plus trying to remember which medications to take when. I often ended up missing appointments or mixing up instructions.

Since having a formal Plan of Care, I feel like everything is mapped out in one place. For example, my PoC clearly lists that I receive a nursing visit once a week on Tuesdays for medication management and wound checks on my left leg, physical therapy twice a week to work on strength and balance, and transportation support for my cardiology and primary care appointments. It also spells out that I have access to a social worker once a month to talk about stress and family issues, which has been really helpful because I sometimes feel guilty asking my adult children for help.

At this 90-day PoC meeting, we reviewed how much progress I’ve made with walking. When I started, I could barely walk from my bedroom to the kitchen without needing to stop. Now, I can walk down the hallway of my apartment building and back with my walker, which is a big deal for me. The PoC meeting allowed my physical therapist and nurse to talk together about adjusting my exercises and making sure my blood pressure is stable enough for me to increase my activity. That kind of coordination is something I could not have managed alone.

Another way the PoC is beneficial is that it provides structure and reassurance. I tend to get anxious when I don’t know what to expect. Having a written plan that we go over in these meetings gives me something to refer back to when I forget details. My case manager printed out a copy and also went over it slowly with me, and now I keep it in a folder in my kitchen drawer. When I feel overwhelmed, I take it out and remind myself that there is a plan, and that I am not just guessing my way through my health issues.

I also appreciate that the PoC meeting gives me a scheduled time to bring up new concerns. For example, in this recent meeting I mentioned that I’ve been feeling more short of breath when climbing the small set of stairs to my building, and that I’ve been having trouble sleeping because of worry about my heart. Because I had this meeting, I knew there was a space to discuss those things, and they weren’t brushed aside. The nurse and case manager added follow-up items to my plan, including checking in with my cardiologist about my medications and referring me to a relaxation and breathing group.

Overall, the PoC and these meetings make my care feel intentional and personalized instead of random. I feel like my health problems are being managed proactively instead of only reacting when something goes wrong.

answers.value:
I do feel included and generally treated as a valued member of the decision-making team, though I would say there is still a little room for improvement, which is why I chose "agree" instead of "strongly agree." The staff are respectful and they do ask for my opinion before finalizing anything in the PoC. For example, when we were discussing how many physical therapy sessions I could realistically handle each week, they did not just tell me what they decided. They asked, “How do you feel about twice a week? Does that feel manageable with your energy level and your other appointments?” That question made me feel like my limits and preferences mattered.

During this 90-day meeting, my case manager made a point of starting by asking me how I felt things were going, before reviewing any of the clinical notes. I appreciated that she didn’t just read off my chart. I was able to say, in my own words, that I felt proud of my progress with walking but still very nervous about having another heart episode. She listened without interrupting and wrote down what I said, and then we talked about how those feelings could be addressed in the plan.

I also feel included because they use language I can understand. I have a high school education but no medical training, so when they use complicated terms, I get lost. In the meeting, the nurse caught herself saying something about “exacerbation of congestive heart failure” and then corrected it to “a flare-up of your heart condition that makes you more short of breath,” which helped a lot. That kind of adjustment shows me they care if I am actually understanding, not just nodding along.

Where I think there could be improvement is in giving me a bit more time to think before decisions are made. Sometimes, the conversation moves quickly, and I later remember a question I wish I had asked. For example, we decided during the meeting to continue weekly nursing visits, but afterwards I wondered if it would be possible to have slightly longer visits less often, so there would be time to talk about lifestyle things like diet, not just medications. I didn’t think of that until the next day. Maybe if they paused more and asked, “Is there anything else you want to think about or come back to?” I would feel even more like a true partner.

Overall though, I do feel listened to. They don’t dismiss my concerns or talk over me, and they always check in by saying things like, “Does this plan sound right to you?” or “Is there anything here you’re not comfortable with?” That makes me feel like I have a real say in my care, not just a symbolic one.

answers.value:
Most of my questions and concerns were addressed by staff during the PoC process, and I generally felt heard. Before the meeting, I wrote down a list of things I wanted to ask about: my shortness of breath when climbing stairs, my anxiety about sleeping alone at night, concerns about a new medication making me feel dizzy, and whether I could get help with transportation to the grocery store once a week instead of every other week.

During the meeting, we went through nearly all of these. The nurse took extra time to explain the possible side effects of my new blood pressure medication and why it might be making me feel lightheaded, especially when standing up quickly. She suggested that I stand up more slowly and keep track of when the dizziness happens, and she promised to call my primary care doctor to see if the dose should be adjusted. That thorough explanation helped ease my fear that something was seriously wrong that we were missing.

We also talked about my shortness of breath. The staff didn’t just say, “That’s part of your condition,” which I’ve heard in other settings. Instead, they asked follow-up questions: When does it happen? How many steps? Do I get chest pain with it? Based on my answers, they decided to add a note to my PoC to schedule a follow-up with my cardiologist sooner than originally planned. They also gave me clear instructions on when to call 911 versus when to call the nurse line, which made me feel safer.

My concern about anxiety at night was also taken seriously. I told them that sometimes I wake up and check my pulse multiple times because I’m scared of having another heart problem in my sleep. The social worker suggested including a referral to a relaxation group and possibly short-term counseling to work on coping skills. They added this to my plan, which made me feel like my emotional health was not being ignored.

The only area where I felt things were not fully addressed was my request about transportation to the grocery store. We discussed it briefly, and the staff said they would “look into it,” but we didn’t come to a clear resolution during the meeting. I understand that some things depend on program rules and availability, but I would have liked a more concrete answer, even if it was, “We can’t do weekly, but we can add one extra trip a month,” or something like that. As of now, that part still feels a little unresolved.

Overall, though, I left the meeting feeling that my main medical and emotional concerns were heard and responded to with specific actions, not just polite words. That makes me more likely to be honest and open in future meetings, because I can see that speaking up actually leads to changes in my care.

answers.value:
I am very comfortable bringing questions and concerns to staff during my PoC meetings and even in between meetings. One of the reasons I feel this way is that from the start, my case manager made it clear that there are no “silly” questions and that my input is not an inconvenience. She gave me her card and the general office number and told me that if something is worrying me, I should call rather than waiting and trying to handle it alone.

In the PoC meetings themselves, the tone is calm and non-judgmental. For example, I admitted during this last meeting that I sometimes forget my evening dose of medication, especially on days when I’m very tired or when my grandchildren visit and the house is busy. I was worried I would get scolded or lectured, but instead, the nurse and case manager thanked me for being honest. They helped me brainstorm practical solutions, like setting an alarm on my phone and using a weekly pill organizer with morning and evening sections. That response made me feel safe to be truthful instead of pretending I’m doing everything perfectly.

Another example is when I brought up something that felt embarrassing: I told them I was having trouble keeping up with some of the home exercises because my knees hurt, and I felt lazy compared to what the physical therapist wanted me to do. Instead of making me feel bad, the physical therapist adjusted the plan and said it was better to do fewer exercises consistently than to push too hard and then stop altogether. That kind of response built trust and made me more willing to speak up when something isn’t working.

I also appreciate that they encourage me to ask for clarification in the moment. When I don’t understand a medical term or a part of the plan, they remind me that it’s okay to say, “Can you explain that again?” or “What does that mean in simple words?” I’ve taken them up on that more than once, and no one has ever made me feel rushed or stupid. That has helped me grow more confident about advocating for myself.

Because of these experiences, I go into PoC meetings feeling like I am allowed to take up space and ask as many questions as I need to. I don’t feel like I have to keep anything to myself out of fear of being judged or dismissed. This is a big change from some of my past experiences in other medical settings, where I felt like just another patient on a long list. Here, I feel like a person whose thoughts and worries matter, and that makes it much easier to bring up concerns early instead of waiting until they become emergencies.
